1: hera'ani YHWH vehineh shnei dudaei te'enim mutzagim lifnei heikhal YHWH (herāʾanî Yhwh wĕhinnēh šĕnê dûdāʾê tĕʾênîm mûṣāgîm lipnê hêkal Yhwh): 'The LORD showed me: there before me were two baskets of figs placed before the temple of the LORD.' The vision is temporally located after the 597 BCE deportation of Jeconiah/Jehoiachin. Dudaei te'enim (baskets of figs) — the temple setting suggests an offering context: figs were among the first-fruits brought to the temple (Deut 26:2-10). The historical background: the 'best people' (craftsmen, officials) were taken in 597, while the weaker Zedekiah and those left behind congratulated themselves on being the surviving remnant.
2: hadud echad te'enim tovot meod kibikure hateenah harishonot vedud echad te'enim ra'ot meod: 'One basket had very good figs, like first-ripe figs, but the other basket had very bad figs, so rotten they could not be eaten.' Bikhure (first-ripe, first-fruits) — the early figs of spring (June) were considered the finest, the most prized (cf. Isa 28:4; Mic 7:1). The comparison 'like first-ripe figs' sets the standard for excellence. The bad figs are not merely of lower quality but ra'ot meod asher lo-ye'akelna (very bad, which cannot be eaten) — completely inedible, spoiled beyond any use.
3: The divine dialogue — 'What do you see, Jeremiah?' / 'Figs' — follows the pattern of the call-visions (1:11-13) where YHWH asks Jeremiah to name what he sees before explaining it. The naming creates engagement: Jeremiah participates in the interpretation process by first observing and describing what is visually obvious (good figs and bad figs) before YHWH supplies the counter-intuitive theological meaning.
4: The formula vayehi dvar YHWH elai lemor (and the word of the LORD came to me, saying) bridges the vision and its interpretation — standard prophetic formula separating the visionary content from the interpretive oracle. The coming word will invert the expected identification: those in exile (who appear to have been punished) are the good figs; those remaining in Judah (who appear to have been spared) are the bad figs.
5: keha-te'enim hatovot ha-elleh ken akir et-galut Yehudah asher shilachti min-hamakom hazeh artza kasdim letovah: 'Like these good figs, I will look favorably on the exiles of Judah whom I sent away from this place to the land of the Chaldeans — for their good.' The counter-intuitive reversal: exile ('sent away') is reinterpreted as divine sending for good. YHWH says 'I sent them' (shilachti) — the exile is not Babylon's victory but YHWH's surgery, removing the best to preserve them for future restoration. Letovah (for their good) — the same phrase YHWH uses to Joseph about his brothers' intentions (Gen 50:20: 'you intended to harm me, but God intended it for good'). The exiles are not being punished; they are being preserved.
6: veshami et-eini aleihem letovah vashevotim el-ha-aretz hazot uvnitim velo-eherros venata'tim velo-etosh: 'I will keep my eyes on them for good, and I will bring them back to this land. I will build them and not tear them down; I will plant them and not uproot them.' The build/uproot language echoes Jeremiah's own call in 1:10: he was appointed 'to pluck up and to break down ... to build and to plant.' Here YHWH uses the same vocabulary but with the negative forms of the destructive verbs: for the exiles, the tearing-down and uprooting phase is complete; only building and planting remain. The four-part structure (build/not-tear-down, plant/not-uproot) mirrors 1:10's six verbs reduced to the positive pair.
7: venatati lahem lev lada'at oti ki-ani YHWH (wĕnātattî lāhem lēb lādaʿat ʾōtî kî-ʾănî Yhwh): 'I will give them a heart to know me, that I am the LORD.' Lev lada'at (a heart to know) is the proto-new-covenant promise: YHWH giving the cognitive and relational faculty of knowing him as a divine gift, not a human achievement. This is the miniature of 31:33-34 ('I will put my Torah in their inner parts and write it on their hearts ... they will all know me from the least to the greatest') and Ezek 36:26 ('I will give you a new heart'). The formula vayehi li le'am va'ani ehyeh lahem le'elohim (they will be my people and I will be their God) is the covenant formula in its most compressed form (cf. 7:23; 11:4; 30:22; 31:33; 32:38; Lev 26:12; Ezek 36:28; 2 Cor 6:16; Rev 21:3). Returning to YHWH with all their heart (bekhol libam) is the response to the given heart — the gift enables the return.
8: ukeha-te'enim hara'ot asher lo-ye'akelna miroa' ken ettein et-tzidqiyahu melech Yehudah: 'But as for the bad figs that are so rotten they cannot be eaten — thus says the LORD — so I will treat Zedekiah king of Judah, his officials, the remnant of Jerusalem who remain in this land, and those who live in the land of Egypt.' The bad-fig identification includes Zedekiah's court (the political establishment left in Judah after 597), the remaining Jerusalemites, and those in Egypt — the community that fled to Egypt against YHWH's explicit instruction (chs. 42-44). The Egyptians-refugees are already anticipated: they chose Egypt over YHWH's direction and are placed with the inedible figs.
9: venatatim lezava'ah lera'ah lechol mamlechot ha'aretz (ûnĕtattîm lĕzawʿāʾ lĕrāʿāh lĕkol mamlĕkôt hāʾāreṣ): 'I will make them a horror to all the kingdoms of the earth — a reproach, a byword, a taunt, and a curse in every place where I drive them.' Four terms of social degradation: zaavah (horror/trembling — causing others to shudder), cherpah (reproach/disgrace), mashal (byword, to be cited as a proverb of shame), and qelalah (curse — the opposite of berakhah). These four echo the comprehensive covenant curses of Deut 28:25, 37 where the same accumulation marks total covenant reversal.
10: veshilachti bam et-hacherev et-hara'av ve'et-hadever (wĕšillaḥtî bām ʾet-haḥereb ʾet-hārāʿāb wĕʾet-haddāber): 'And I will send the sword, famine, and pestilence against them until they are completely destroyed from the land I gave to them and to their fathers.' The triad of sword (cherev), famine (ra'av), and pestilence (dever) is Jeremiah's signature covenant-judgment formula — appearing at least twelve times in the book (14:12; 21:7, 9; 27:8, 13; 28:8; 29:17-18; 32:24, 36; 34:17; 38:2; 44:13). These are the three classic covenant curses of Lev 26 and Deut 28 condensed into a triad, representing military defeat, agricultural failure, and plague. Their application to those remaining in the land makes the exile-survival the superior outcome: being taken captive to Babylon is better than facing the triad in Judah.
